diagrams.title: Three-Factor Authentication Model
diagrams.description: A visual representation of the three authentication factors: Something You Know (knowledge), Something You Have (possession), and Something You Are (inherence). Each factor is shown with common examples like passwords, security tokens, and biometrics.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental concept that strong authentication requires combining multiple independent factors to verify user identity.

diagrams.title: Authentication Process Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart-style diagram showing the step-by-step authentication process from user login request through identity verification to access control decision (grant or deny).
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the complete authentication lifecycle and how systems verify user credentials before granting access to protected resources.

diagrams.title: Authentication Methods Comparison
diagrams.description: A comparative visual of different authentication methods including something you know (password, security questions), something you have (hardware token, software token, certificate), and something you are (biometrics). Shows relative security levels.
diagrams.purpose: Compares various authentication mechanisms to help understand their strengths, weaknesses, and appropriate use cases in different security scenarios.
